# Reference

<!-- DO NOT EDIT: This document was generated by Puppet Strings -->

## Table of Contents

### Classes

* [`opn`](#opn): Manages OPNsense firewalls via the REST API.
* [`opn::client`](#opn--client): Exports OPNsense resources for collection by the management server.
* [`opn::config`](#opn--config): Manages provider configuration files for OPNsense API access.

### Defined types

* [`opn::device_config`](#opn--device_config): Creates a single OPNsense device credential file.

## Classes

### <a name="opn"></a>`opn`

This class is the main entry point for the puppet-opn module. It delegates
provider configuration (config directory, credential files) to opn::config
and creates opn_* resources for one or more OPNsense devices.

#### Examples

##### Single device with plugins and aliases

```puppet
class { 'opn':
  devices => {
    'opnsense.example.com' => {
      'url'        => 'https://opnsense.example.com/api',
      'api_key'    => 'your_api_key_here',
      'api_secret' => 'your_api_secret_here',
      'ssl_verify' => true,
    },
  },
  plugins => {
    'os-haproxy' => {
      'devices' => ['opnsense.example.com'],
      'ensure'  => 'present',
    },
  },
  firewall_aliases => {
    'http_ports' => {
      'devices' => ['opnsense.example.com'],
      'ensure'  => 'present',
      'config'  => {
        'type'        => 'port',
        'content'     => '80,443',
        'description' => 'HTTP(S) ports',
        'enabled'     => '1',
      },
    },
  },
}
```

##### <a name="-opn--acmeclient_accounts"></a>`acmeclient_accounts`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of ACME Client accounts to manage across devices.
Each key is the account name.
Each value is a hash with:
  - devices [Array] List of device names. Defaults to all devices.
  - ensure  [String] 'present' or 'absent' (default: 'present')
  - config  [Hash] Configuration hash passed to opn_acmeclient_account.

##### <a name="-opn--acmeclient_actions"></a>`acmeclient_actions`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of ACME Client automation actions to manage across devices.
Each key is the action name.
Each value is a hash with:
  - devices [Array] List of device names. Defaults to all devices.
  - ensure  [String] 'present' or 'absent' (default: 'present')
  - config  [Hash] Configuration hash passed to opn_acmeclient_action.

##### <a name="-opn--acmeclient_certificates"></a>`acmeclient_certificates`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of ACME Client certificates to manage across devices.
Each key is the certificate name.
Each value is a hash with:
  - devices [Array] List of device names. Defaults to all devices.
  - ensure  [String] 'present' or 'absent' (default: 'present')
  - config  [Hash] Configuration hash passed to opn_acmeclient_certificate.

##### <a name="-opn--acmeclient_settings"></a>`acmeclient_settings`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of ACME Client global settings, one per device.
Each key is the device name (not a "name@device" title).
Each value is a hash with:
  - ensure  [String] 'present' or 'absent' (default: 'present')
  - config  [Hash] Configuration hash passed to opn_acmeclient_settings.

##### <a name="-opn--acmeclient_validations"></a>`acmeclient_validations`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of ACME Client validation methods to manage across devices.
Each key is the validation method name.
Each value is a hash with:
  - devices [Array] List of device names. Defaults to all devices.
  - ensure  [String] 'present' or 'absent' (default: 'present')
  - config  [Hash] Configuration hash passed to opn_acmeclient_validation.

##### <a name="-opn--additional_devices"></a>`additional_devices`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of additional OPNsense devices that only need API credential files.
Unlike $devices, these devices are NOT included in the default device list
for resource iteration. Use this for remote devices that need credentials
but should not have all resources managed on them via the main class.
Each key is the device name, each value is a hash with connection parameters
(url, api_key, api_secret, ssl_verify, timeout).

##### <a name="-opn--cron_jobs"></a>`cron_jobs`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of cron jobs to manage across devices.
Each key is the cron job description.
Each value is a hash with:
  - devices [Array] List of device names. Defaults to all devices.
  - ensure  [String] 'present' or 'absent' (default: 'present')
  - config  [Hash] Configuration hash passed to opn_cron.

##### <a name="-opn--dhcrelay_destinations"></a>`dhcrelay_destinations`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of DHCP Relay destinations to manage across devices.
Each key is the destination name.
Each value is a hash with:
  - devices [Array] List of device names. Defaults to all devices.
  - ensure  [String] 'present' or 'absent' (default: 'present')
  - config  [Hash] Configuration hash passed to opn_dhcrelay_destination.

##### <a name="-opn--dhcrelays"></a>`dhcrelays`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of DHCP Relay instances to manage across devices.
Each key is a freeform label (not sent to the API).
Each value is a hash with:
  - devices [Array] List of device names. Defaults to all devices.
  - ensure  [String] 'present' or 'absent' (default: 'present')
  - config  [Hash] Configuration hash passed to opn_dhcrelay.

##### <a name="-opn--devices"></a>`devices`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of OPNsense devices to manage. Each key is the device name used as
suffix in opn_* resource titles (format: "resource@device_name").
Each value is a hash with connection parameters:
  - url        [String]  OPNsense API base URL
  - api_key    [String]  OPNsense API key (required)
  - api_secret [String]  OPNsense API secret (required)
  - ssl_verify [Boolean] Verify SSL certificate (default: true)
  - timeout    [Integer] HTTP timeout in seconds (default: 60)

##### <a name="-opn--firewall_aliases"></a>`firewall_aliases`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of firewall aliases to manage across devices.
Each key is the alias name.
Each value is a hash with:
  - devices [Array] List of device names. Defaults to all devices.
  - ensure  [String] 'present' or 'absent' (default: 'present')
  - config  [Hash] Configuration hash passed to opn_firewall_alias.

##### <a name="-opn--firewall_categories"></a>`firewall_categories`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of firewall categories to manage across devices.
Each key is the category name.
Each value is a hash with:
  - devices [Array] List of device names. Defaults to all devices.
  - ensure  [String] 'present' or 'absent' (default: 'present')
  - config  [Hash] Configuration hash passed to opn_firewall_category.

##### <a name="-opn--firewall_groups"></a>`firewall_groups`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of firewall interface groups to manage across devices.
Each key is the interface group name (ifname).
Each value is a hash with:
  - devices [Array] List of device names. Defaults to all devices.
  - ensure  [String] 'present' or 'absent' (default: 'present')
  - config  [Hash] Configuration hash passed to opn_firewall_group.

##### <a name="-opn--firewall_rules"></a>`firewall_rules`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of firewall filter rules to manage across devices.
Each key is the rule description (must be unique per device).
Each value is a hash with:
  - devices [Array] List of device names. Defaults to all devices.
  - ensure  [String] 'present' or 'absent' (default: 'present')
  - config  [Hash] Configuration hash passed to opn_firewall_rule.

##### <a name="-opn--gateways"></a>`gateways`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of gateways to manage across devices.
Each key is the gateway name (e.g. 'WAN_GW').
Each value is a hash with:
  - devices [Array] List of device names. Defaults to all devices.
  - ensure  [String] 'present' or 'absent' (default: 'present')
  - config  [Hash] Configuration hash passed to opn_gateway.

##### <a name="-opn--groups"></a>`groups`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of local groups to manage across devices.
Each key is the group name.
Each value is a hash with:
  - devices [Array] List of device names. Defaults to all devices.
  - ensure  [String] 'present' or 'absent' (default: 'present')
  - config  [Hash] Configuration hash passed to opn_group.

##### <a name="-opn--manage_resources"></a>`manage_resources`

Data type: `Boolean`

Boolean to enable collection of exported opn_* resources from client nodes
via PuppetDB. When true, the class collects all exported opn_* resources
tagged with the device name. Default: false.

### <a name="opn--client"></a>`opn::client`

This class is used on client nodes (e.g. application servers) to declare
exported opn_* resources. The management server running the opn class with
manage_resources => true collects and applies these resources via PuppetDB.

Each resource item must include a 'devices' key listing the target OPNsense
device names. Unlike the opn class, there is no global devices list to
fall back to.

Singleton types (opn_acmeclient_settings, opn_haproxy_settings, opn_hasync,
opn_zabbix_agent, opn_zabbix_proxy) are excluded because they represent
per-device global settings that should only be managed by the opn class on
the management server.

#### Examples

##### Export a firewall alias from a client node

```puppet
class { 'opn::client':
  firewall_aliases => {
    'webserver_ips' => {
      'devices' => ['opnsense01.example.com'],
      'config'  => {
        'type'        => 'host',
        'content'     => $facts['networking']['ip'],
        'description' => "${facts['networking']['fqdn']} - Web server IPs",
        'enabled'     => '1',
      },
    },
  },
}
```

* [`acmeclient_actions`](#-opn--client--acmeclient_actions)

##### <a name="-opn--client--acmeclient_accounts"></a>`acmeclient_accounts`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of ACME Client accounts to export.
Each key is the account name.
Each value is a hash with:
  - devices [Array] List of target device names (mandatory).
  - ensure  [String] 'present' or 'absent' (default: 'present')
  - config  [Hash] Configuration hash passed to opn_acmeclient_account.

##### <a name="-opn--client--acmeclient_actions"></a>`acmeclient_actions`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of ACME Client automation actions to export.
Each key is the action name.
Each value is a hash with:
  - devices [Array] List of target device names (mandatory).
  - ensure  [String] 'present' or 'absent' (default: 'present')
  - config  [Hash] Configuration hash passed to opn_acmeclient_action.

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of ACME Client certificates to export.
Each key is the certificate name.
Each value is a hash with:
  - devices [Array] List of target device names (mandatory).
  - ensure  [String] 'present' or 'absent' (default: 'present')
  - config  [Hash] Configuration hash passed to opn_acmeclient_certificate.

  - config  [Hash] Configuration hash passed to opn_acmeclient_validation.

### <a name="opn--config"></a>`opn::config`

Creates the config directory, per-device credential files, and a provider
config file that tells the Ruby providers where to find device credentials.

This class can be used standalone to set up provider credentials without
managing any OPNsense resources via the main opn class.

#### Examples

##### Standalone usage

```puppet
class { 'opn::config':
  devices => {
    'opnsense01' => {
      'url'        => 'https://opnsense01.example.com/api',
      'api_key'    => 'key',
      'api_secret' => 'secret',
    },
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn::config` class:

* [`config_dir`](#-opn--config--config_dir)
* [`devices`](#-opn--config--devices)
* [`owner`](#-opn--config--owner)
* [`group`](#-opn--config--group)

##### <a name="-opn--config--config_dir"></a>`config_dir`

Data type: `Stdlib::Absolutepath`

Directory where per-device YAML credential files are stored.

##### <a name="-opn--config--devices"></a>`devices`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash of OPNsense devices. Each key is the device name, each value is a
hash with connection parameters (url, api_key, api_secret, ssl_verify, timeout).

##### <a name="-opn--config--owner"></a>`owner`

Data type: `String`

Owner of the config directory and credential files.

##### <a name="-opn--config--group"></a>`group`

Data type: `String`

Group of the config directory and credential files.

## Defined types

### <a name="opn--device_config"></a>`opn::device_config`

Writes a YAML file containing API credentials for one OPNsense device.
The file is restricted to mode 0600 and diff output is suppressed to
protect sensitive data.

#### Examples

##### 

```puppet
opn::device_config { 'opnsense01':
  config_dir    => '/etc/puppet/opn',
  device_config => {
    'url'        => 'https://opnsense01.example.com/api',
    'api_key'    => 'key',
    'api_secret' => 'secret',
  },
  group         => 'root',
  owner         => 'root',
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn::device_config` defined type:

* [`config_dir`](#-opn--device_config--config_dir)
* [`device_config`](#-opn--device_config--device_config)
* [`group`](#-opn--device_config--group)
* [`owner`](#-opn--device_config--owner)

##### <a name="-opn--device_config--config_dir"></a>`config_dir`

Data type: `Stdlib::Absolutepath`

Directory where per-device YAML credential files are stored.

##### <a name="-opn--device_config--device_config"></a>`device_config`

Data type: `Hash`

Hash with connection parameters (url, api_key, api_secret, ssl_verify, timeout).

##### <a name="-opn--device_config--group"></a>`group`

Data type: `String`

Group of the credential file.

##### <a name="-opn--device_config--owner"></a>`owner`

Data type: `String`

Owner of the credential file.

## Resource types

### <a name="opn_acmeclient_account"></a>`opn_acmeclient_account`

Manages ACME Client accounts on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense
REST API (os-acme-client plugin).

The resource title uses the format "name@device_name", where "name"
uniquely identifies the ACME account and "device_name" corresponds to
a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

Volatile fields (key, statusCode, statusLastUpdate) are only relevant
after account registration and are excluded from idempotency checks.

All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API.

#### Examples

##### Register a Let's Encrypt account

```puppet
opn_acmeclient_account { 'le-account@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'ca'               => 'letsencrypt',
    'email'            => 'admin@example.com',
    'enabled'          => '1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_acmeclient_account` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_acmeclient_account--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_acmeclient_account--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_acmeclient_account` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet
will usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_acmeclient_action"></a>`opn_acmeclient_action`

Manages ACME Client automation actions on an OPNsense device via the
OPNsense REST API (os-acme-client plugin).

The resource title uses the format "name@device_name", where "name"
uniquely identifies the action and "device_name" corresponds to a YAML
config file managed by the opn class at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API.

#### Examples

##### Create a restart action

```puppet
opn_acmeclient_action { 'restart_haproxy@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'type'    => 'configd',
    'configd' => 'haproxy restart',
    'enabled' => '1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_acmeclient_action` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_acmeclient_action--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_acmeclient_action--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_acmeclient_action` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet
will usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_acmeclient_certificate"></a>`opn_acmeclient_certificate`

Manages ACME Client certificates on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense
REST API (os-acme-client plugin).

The resource title uses the format "name@device_name", where "name"
uniquely identifies the certificate and "device_name" corresponds to
a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

Relation fields (account, validationMethod, restartActions) accept names
which are automatically resolved to UUIDs via the IdResolver.

Volatile fields (certRefId, lastUpdate, statusCode, statusLastUpdate)
are excluded from idempotency checks.

#### Examples

##### Request a certificate

```puppet
opn_acmeclient_certificate { 'web.example.com@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'altNames'         => 'www.example.com',
    'account'          => 'le-account',
    'validationMethod' => 'http-01',
    'restartActions'   => 'restart_haproxy',
    'enabled'          => '1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_acmeclient_certificate` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_acmeclient_certificate--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_acmeclient_certificate--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_acmeclient_certificate` resource. You will seldom need to specify this ---
Puppet will usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_acmeclient_settings"></a>`opn_acmeclient_settings`

Manages ACME Client global settings on an OPNsense device via the
OPNsense REST API (os-acme-client plugin).

The resource title is the OPNsense device name, corresponding to a YAML
config file managed by the opn class at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

This is a singleton resource -- one per OPNsense device. The `config` hash
is passed directly to the OPNsense API without modification.

Relation fields (UpdateCron, haproxyAclRef, haproxyActionRef,
haproxyServerRef, haproxyBackendRef) accept names which are automatically
resolved to UUIDs.

After any settings change, Puppet calls `acmeclient/service/reconfigure`
once per device.

#### Examples

##### Configure ACME Client settings

```puppet
opn_acmeclient_settings { 'opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'environment' => 'stg',
    'logLevel'    => 'normal',
    'autoRenewal' => '1',
    'UpdateCron'  => 'ACME renew cron',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_acmeclient_settings` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_acmeclient_settings--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_acmeclient_settings--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_acmeclient_settings` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet
will usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_acmeclient_validation"></a>`opn_acmeclient_validation`

Manages ACME Client validation methods on an OPNsense device via the
OPNsense REST API (os-acme-client plugin).

The resource title uses the format "name@device_name", where "name"
uniquely identifies the validation method and "device_name" corresponds
to a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

The relation field http_haproxyFrontends accepts HAProxy frontend names
which are automatically resolved to UUIDs.

All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API.

#### Examples

##### Create an HTTP-01 validation method

```puppet
opn_acmeclient_validation { 'http-01@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'method'               => 'http01',
    'http_service'         => 'haproxy',
    'http_haproxyFrontends'=> 'https_frontend',
    'enabled'              => '1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_acmeclient_validation` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_acmeclient_validation--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_acmeclient_validation--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_acmeclient_validation` resource. You will seldom need to specify this ---
Puppet will usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_cron"></a>`opn_cron`

Manages cron jobs on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "description@device_name", where
"description" uniquely identifies the cron job and "device_name" corresponds
to a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

Note: The description must be unique per device. Two cron jobs with the same
description on the same device will cause unpredictable behaviour.

All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The `config`
hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Create a daily reload cron job

```puppet
opn_cron { 'Daily haproxy reload@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'command'  => 'haproxy reload',
    'minutes'  => '0',
    'hours'    => '3',
    'days'     => '*',
    'months'   => '*',
    'weekdays' => '*',
    'enabled'  => '1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_cron` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_cron--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_cron--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_cron` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will usually
discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_dhcrelay"></a>`opn_dhcrelay`

Manages DHCP Relay instances on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "label@device_name", where "label" is a
freeform identifier chosen by the user (it is NOT sent to the API) and
"device_name" corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

Important: DHCP Relay instances have no name/description field in the
OPNsense API. The provider matches existing API resources by the
`interface` value from the config hash. Each interface can only have one
relay per device.

The `destination` config key accepts a destination name which is
automatically resolved to the corresponding UUID by the provider.

All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The `config`
hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Create a DHCP Relay on the LAN interface

```puppet
opn_dhcrelay { 'LAN IPv4 Relay@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'interface'   => 'lan',
    'destination' => 'DHCP Servers',
    'enabled'     => '1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_dhcrelay` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_dhcrelay--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_dhcrelay--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_dhcrelay` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_dhcrelay_destination"></a>`opn_dhcrelay_destination`

Manages DHCP Relay destinations on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "name@device_name", where
"name" uniquely identifies the DHCP Relay destination and "device_name"
corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

Note: The name must be unique per device. Two DHCP Relay destinations
with the same name on the same device will cause unpredictable
behaviour.

All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The `config`
hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Create a DHCP Relay destination

```puppet
opn_dhcrelay_destination { 'DHCP Servers@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'server' => '10.0.0.1,10.0.0.2',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_dhcrelay_destination` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_dhcrelay_destination--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_dhcrelay_destination--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_dhcrelay_destination` resource. You will seldom need to specify this ---
Puppet will usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_firewall_alias"></a>`opn_firewall_alias`

Manages firewall aliases on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "alias_name@device_name", where
"device_name" corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class
at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The `config`
hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Port alias

```puppet
opn_firewall_alias { 'http_ports@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'type'        => 'port',
    'content'     => '80,443',
    'description' => 'HTTP(S) ports',
    'enabled'     => '1',
  },
}
```

##### Network alias

```puppet
opn_firewall_alias { 'mgmt_nets@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'type'    => 'network',
    'content' => '10.0.0.0/8\n192.168.0.0/16',
    'enabled' => '1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_firewall_alias` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_firewall_alias--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_firewall_alias--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_firewall_alias` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_firewall_category"></a>`opn_firewall_category`

Manages firewall categories on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "category_name@device_name", where
"device_name" corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class
at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

Firewall categories are used to group and organise rules, aliases, and other
firewall objects. All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API.

#### Examples

##### Create a firewall category

```puppet
opn_firewall_category { 'web_traffic@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'color' => '0088cc',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_firewall_category` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_firewall_category--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_firewall_category--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_firewall_category` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet
will usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_firewall_group"></a>`opn_firewall_group`

Manages firewall interface groups on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "ifname@device_name", where "ifname" is the
interface group name and "device_name" corresponds to a YAML config file managed
by the opn class at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

System-managed interface groups (e.g. enc0/IPsec, openvpn, wireguard) are not
managed by this type and are automatically skipped during catalog prefetch.

All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The `config`
hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Create a firewall interface group

```puppet
opn_firewall_group { 'dmz_servers@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'members'  => 'em1,em2',
    'descr'    => 'DMZ server interfaces',
    'sequence' => '10',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_firewall_group` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_firewall_group--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_firewall_group--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_firewall_group` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_firewall_rule"></a>`opn_firewall_rule`

Manages firewall filter rules on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "description@device_name", where
"description" uniquely identifies the rule and "device_name" corresponds to a
YAML config file managed by the opn class at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

Note: The description must be unique per device. Two rules with the same
description on the same device will cause unpredictable behaviour.

All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The `config`
hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Allow HTTP and HTTPS traffic

```puppet
opn_firewall_rule { 'Allow HTTP(S)@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'action'          => 'pass',
    'interface'       => 'lan',
    'ipprotocol'      => 'inet',
    'protocol'        => 'tcp',
    'source_net'      => 'any',
    'destination_net' => 'any',
    'destination_port'=> '80,443',
    'enabled'         => '1',
  },
}
```

##### Block traffic from a specific host

```puppet
opn_firewall_rule { 'Block suspicious host@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'action'     => 'block',
    'interface'  => 'wan',
    'source_net' => '203.0.113.42',
    'enabled'    => '1',
    'log'        => '1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_firewall_rule` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_firewall_rule--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_firewall_rule--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_firewall_rule` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_gateway"></a>`opn_gateway`

Manages gateways on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "name@device_name", where "name"
uniquely identifies the gateway (e.g. "WAN_GW") and "device_name"
corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

Note: The gateway name must be unique per device and cannot be changed
after creation (OPNsense restriction). The name may only consist of the
characters "a-zA-Z0-9_-" and must be less than 32 characters long.

All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The `config`
hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Manage a gateway

```puppet
opn_gateway { 'WAN_GW@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'interface'       => 'wan',
    'ipprotocol'      => 'inet',
    'gateway'         => '192.168.1.1',
    'descr'           => 'WAN Gateway',
    'monitor_disable' => '1',
    'priority'        => '255',
    'weight'          => '1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_gateway` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_gateway--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_gateway--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_gateway` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_group"></a>`opn_group`

Manages local groups on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "groupname@device_name", where
"device_name" corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class
at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The `config`
hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Create a local group

```puppet
opn_group { 'vpn_users@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'description'    => 'VPN Users',
    'priv'           => 'page-dashboard',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_group` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_group--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_group--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_group` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will usually
discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_haproxy_acl"></a>`opn_haproxy_acl`

Manages HAProxy ACL rules on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "name@device_name". The "name" portion
(before "@") is the identifier sent to the OPNsense API and must be unique
per device. It is always set from the title — specifying a different "name"
value inside the `config` hash has no effect.

To rename a resource, rename the resource title. If an existing entry must
be renamed, declare the old title with `ensure => absent` and add a new
resource with the new title.

All other configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The
`config` hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Define an ACL

```puppet
opn_haproxy_acl { 'is_https@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'expression'  => 'ssl_fc',
    'description' => 'SSL/TLS connection check',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_haproxy_acl` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_haproxy_acl--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_haproxy_acl--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_haproxy_acl` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_haproxy_action"></a>`opn_haproxy_action`

Manages HAProxy actions (rule actions) on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "name@device_name". The "name" portion
(before "@") is the identifier sent to the OPNsense API and must be unique
per device. It is always set from the title — specifying a different "name"
value inside the `config` hash has no effect.

To rename a resource, rename the resource title. If an existing entry must
be renamed, declare the old title with `ensure => absent` and add a new
resource with the new title.

All other configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The
`config` hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Define an action

```puppet
opn_haproxy_action { 'redirect_to_https@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'type'        => 'redirect',
    'description' => 'Redirect HTTP to HTTPS',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_haproxy_action` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_haproxy_action--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_haproxy_action--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_haproxy_action` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_haproxy_backend"></a>`opn_haproxy_backend`

Manages HAProxy backend pools on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "name@device_name". The "name" portion
(before "@") is the identifier sent to the OPNsense API and must be unique
per device. It is always set from the title — specifying a different "name"
value inside the `config` hash has no effect.

To rename a resource, rename the resource title. If an existing entry must
be renamed, declare the old title with `ensure => absent` and add a new
resource with the new title.

All other configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The
`config` hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Define a backend pool

```puppet
opn_haproxy_backend { 'web_backend@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'mode'        => 'http',
    'description' => 'Web server backend pool',
    'enabled'     => '1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_haproxy_backend` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_haproxy_backend--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_haproxy_backend--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_haproxy_backend` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet
will usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_haproxy_cpu"></a>`opn_haproxy_cpu`

Manages HAProxy CPU affinity / thread binding on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "name@device_name". The "name" portion
(before "@") is the identifier sent to the OPNsense API and must be unique
per device. It is always set from the title — specifying a different "name"
value inside the `config` hash has no effect.

To rename a resource, rename the resource title. If an existing entry must
be renamed, declare the old title with `ensure => absent` and add a new
resource with the new title.

All other configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The
`config` hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Define CPU affinity

```puppet
opn_haproxy_cpu { 'cpu0@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'description' => 'CPU 0 binding',
  },
}
```

### <a name="opn_haproxy_errorfile"></a>`opn_haproxy_errorfile`

Manages HAProxy error files on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "name@device_name". The "name" portion
(before "@") is the identifier sent to the OPNsense API and must be unique
per device. It is always set from the title — specifying a different "name"
value inside the `config` hash has no effect.

To rename a resource, rename the resource title. If an existing entry must
be renamed, declare the old title with `ensure => absent` and add a new
resource with the new title.

All other configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The
`config` hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Define an error file

```puppet
opn_haproxy_errorfile { 'custom_503@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'code'        => '503',
    'description' => 'Service Unavailable',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_haproxy_errorfile` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_haproxy_errorfile--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_haproxy_errorfile--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_haproxy_errorfile` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet
will usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_haproxy_settings"></a>`opn_haproxy_settings`

Manages HAProxy global settings on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense
REST API.

The resource title is the OPNsense device name, corresponding to a YAML
config file managed by the opn class at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

This is a singleton resource -- one per OPNsense device. It manages the
HAProxy plugin's 'general' and 'maintenance' sections. The `config` hash
is passed directly to the OPNsense API without modification.

Cron job references in maintenance.cronjobs are resolved by description.
User/group references in general.stats are resolved by name.

Requires the os-haproxy plugin to be installed on the device.

#### Examples

##### Configure HAProxy global settings

```puppet
opn_haproxy_settings { 'opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'general' => {
      'enabled' => '1',
      'stats'   => {
        'enabled'       => '1',
        'allowedUsers'  => 'haproxy_admin',
        'allowedGroups' => 'stats_viewers',
      },
    },
    'maintenance' => {
      'cronjobs' => {
        'syncCertsCron' => 'HAProxy: sync certificates',
      },
    },
  },
}
```

##### Disable HAProxy

```puppet
opn_haproxy_settings { 'opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => absent,
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_haproxy_settings` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_haproxy_settings--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_haproxy_settings--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_haproxy_settings` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet
will usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

OPNsense REST API.

The resource title is the OPNsense device name, corresponding to a YAML
config file managed by the opn class at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

This is a singleton resource -- one per OPNsense device. The `config` hash
is passed directly to the OPNsense API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Configure HA sync

```puppet
opn_hasync { 'opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'pfsyncenabled'    => '1',
    'pfsyncinterface'  => 'lan',
    'synchronizetoip'  => '10.0.0.2',
    'username'         => 'root',
    'password'         => 'secret',
  },
}
```

##### Disable HA sync

```puppet
opn_hasync { 'opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => absent,
}
```

#### Parameters

* [`provider`](#-opn_hasync--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_hasync--provider"></a>`provider`

discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_ipsec_child"></a>`opn_ipsec_child`

Manages IPsec child SA (Security Association) entries on an OPNsense
device via the OPNsense REST API (Swanctl/MVC model).

The resource title uses the format "description@device_name", where
"description" uniquely identifies the child SA and "device_name"
corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

The connection relation field accepts a connection description which is
automatically resolved to a UUID via the IdResolver.

#### Examples

##### Create an IPsec child SA

```puppet
opn_ipsec_child { 'child-lan@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'connection'    => 'site-to-site',
    'mode'          => 'tunnel',
    'local_ts'      => '10.0.0.0/24',
    'remote_ts'     => '10.0.1.0/24',
    'esp_proposals' => 'aes256-sha256',
    'enabled'       => '1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_ipsec_child` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_ipsec_child--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_ipsec_child--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_ipsec_child` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_ipsec_connection"></a>`opn_ipsec_connection`

Manages IPsec connections on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API
(Swanctl/MVC model).

The resource title uses the format "description@device_name", where
"description" uniquely identifies the connection and "device_name"
corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

Volatile fields (local_ts, remote_ts) are computed from children and
excluded from idempotency checks.

#### Examples

##### Create an IPsec connection

```puppet
opn_ipsec_connection { 'site-to-site@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'version'    => '2',
    'proposals'  => 'aes256-sha256-modp2048',
    'local_addrs' => '0.0.0.0/0',
    'remote_addrs' => '198.51.100.1',
    'enabled'    => '1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_ipsec_connection` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_ipsec_connection--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_ipsec_connection--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_ipsec_connection` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet
will usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_ipsec_keypair"></a>`opn_ipsec_keypair`

Manages IPsec key pairs on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API
(Swanctl/MVC model).

The resource title uses the format "name@device_name", where "name"
uniquely identifies the key pair and "device_name" corresponds to a YAML
config file managed by the opn class at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

The 'privateKey' field is intentionally excluded from idempotency
comparison because it contains secret key material.

Volatile fields (keyFingerprint, keySize) are computed by OPNsense and
excluded from idempotency checks.

#### Examples

##### Create an IPsec key pair

```puppet
opn_ipsec_keypair { 'my-keypair@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'keyType'    => 'rsa',
    'keySize'    => '2048',
    'privateKey' => '-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----...',
    'publicKey'  => '-----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY-----...',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_ipsec_keypair` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_ipsec_keypair--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_ipsec_keypair--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_ipsec_keypair` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_ipsec_local"></a>`opn_ipsec_local`

Manages IPsec local authentication entries on an OPNsense device via the
OPNsense REST API (Swanctl/MVC model).

The resource title uses the format "description@device_name", where
"description" uniquely identifies the local auth entry and "device_name"
corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

Relation fields (connection, pubkeys) accept names which are automatically
resolved to UUIDs via the IdResolver.

#### Examples

##### Create an IPsec local authentication

```puppet
opn_ipsec_local { 'local-auth@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'connection' => 'site-to-site',
    'auth'       => 'pubkey',
    'id'         => 'CN=opnsense.example.com',
    'pubkeys'    => 'my-keypair',
    'enabled'    => '1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_ipsec_local` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_ipsec_local--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_ipsec_local--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_ipsec_local` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

Manages IPsec address pools on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense
REST API (Swanctl/MVC model).

The resource title uses the format "name@device_name", where "name"
uniquely identifies the pool and "device_name" corresponds to a YAML

#### Examples

##### Create an IPsec pool

```puppet
opn_ipsec_pool { 'vpn-pool@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'addrs' => '10.10.0.0/24',
    'dns'   => '10.10.0.1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

* [`provider`](#-opn_ipsec_pool--provider)

The specific backend to use for this `opn_ipsec_pool` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_ipsec_presharedkey"></a>`opn_ipsec_presharedkey`

Manages IPsec pre-shared keys on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense
REST API (Swanctl/MVC model).

The resource title uses the format "ident@device_name", where "ident"
uniquely identifies the pre-shared key and "device_name" corresponds to
a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

The 'Key' field is intentionally excluded from idempotency comparison
because it contains the actual pre-shared key secret.

#### Examples

##### Create an IPsec pre-shared key

```puppet
opn_ipsec_presharedkey { 'remote-peer@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'keyType' => 'PSK',
    'Key'     => 'supersecretkey',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_ipsec_presharedkey` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_ipsec_presharedkey--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_ipsec_presharedkey--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_ipsec_presharedkey` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet
will usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_ipsec_remote"></a>`opn_ipsec_remote`

Manages IPsec remote authentication entries on an OPNsense device via the
OPNsense REST API (Swanctl/MVC model).

The resource title uses the format "description@device_name", where
"description" uniquely identifies the remote auth entry and "device_name"
corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

Relation fields (connection, pubkeys) accept names which are automatically
resolved to UUIDs via the IdResolver.

#### Examples

##### Create an IPsec remote authentication

```puppet
opn_ipsec_remote { 'remote-auth@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'connection' => 'site-to-site',
    'auth'       => 'pubkey',
    'id'         => 'CN=remote.example.com',
    'pubkeys'    => 'remote-keypair',
    'enabled'    => '1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_ipsec_remote` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_ipsec_remote--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_ipsec_remote--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_ipsec_remote` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_ipsec_settings"></a>`opn_ipsec_settings`

Manages IPsec global settings on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense
REST API (Swanctl/MVC model).

The resource title is the OPNsense device name, corresponding to a YAML
config file managed by the opn class at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

This is a singleton resource -- one per OPNsense device. It manages the
IPsec 'general' and 'charon' sections. The `config` hash is passed
directly to the OPNsense API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Configure IPsec global settings

```puppet
opn_ipsec_settings { 'opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'general' => {
      'enabled' => '1',
    },
    'charon' => {
      'threads' => '16',
    },
  },
}
```

##### Disable IPsec

```puppet
opn_ipsec_settings { 'opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => absent,
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_ipsec_settings` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_ipsec_settings--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_ipsec_settings--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_ipsec_settings` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_ipsec_vti"></a>`opn_ipsec_vti`

Manages IPsec VTI (Virtual Tunnel Interface) entries on an OPNsense
device via the OPNsense REST API (Swanctl/MVC model).

The resource title uses the format "description@device_name", where
"description" uniquely identifies the VTI and "device_name" corresponds
to a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

Volatile fields (origin) are computed by OPNsense and excluded from
idempotency checks.

#### Examples

##### Create an IPsec VTI

```puppet
opn_ipsec_vti { 'tunnel-to-remote@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'reqid'       => '100',
    'local'       => '10.0.0.1',
    'remote'      => '10.0.0.2',
    'tunnel_local'  => '10.10.0.1/30',
    'tunnel_remote' => '10.10.0.2/30',
    'enabled'     => '1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_ipsec_vti` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_ipsec_vti--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_ipsec_vti--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_ipsec_vti` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_kea_dhcpv4"></a>`opn_kea_dhcpv4`

Manages KEA DHCPv4 global settings on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense
REST API.

The resource title is the OPNsense device name, corresponding to a YAML
config file managed by the opn class at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

This is a singleton resource -- one per OPNsense device. It manages the
KEA DHCPv4 'general', 'lexpire' and 'ha' sections. The `config` hash
is passed directly to the OPNsense API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Configure KEA DHCPv4 settings

```puppet
opn_kea_dhcpv4 { 'opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'general' => {
      'enabled'          => '1',
      'interfaces'       => 'lan',
      'valid_lifetime'   => '4000',
      'fwrules'          => '1',
      'dhcp_socket_type' => 'raw',
    },
    'lexpire' => {
      'reclaim_timer_wait_time' => '10',
    },
    'ha' => {
      'enabled' => '0',
    },
  },
}
```

##### Disable KEA DHCPv4

```puppet
opn_kea_dhcpv4 { 'opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => absent,
}
```

#### Parameters

* [`provider`](#-opn_kea_dhcpv4--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_kea_dhcpv4--provider"></a>`provider`

Manages KEA DHCPv4 HA peers on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "name@device_name", where "name"
uniquely identifies the HA peer and "device_name" corresponds to a YAML

```puppet
  },

```

The following parameters are available in the `opn_kea_dhcpv4_peer` type.

### <a name="opn_kea_dhcpv4_reservation"></a>`opn_kea_dhcpv4_reservation`

Manages KEA DHCPv4 reservations on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense
REST API.

The resource title uses the format "description@device_name", where
"description" uniquely identifies the reservation and "device_name"
corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

The subnet relation field accepts a subnet CIDR which is automatically
resolved to a UUID via the IdResolver.

#### Examples

##### Create a DHCPv4 reservation

```puppet
opn_kea_dhcpv4_reservation { 'Web Server@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'subnet'     => '192.168.1.0/24',
    'hw_address' => 'AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF',
    'ip_address' => '192.168.1.10',
    'hostname'   => 'webserver',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_kea_dhcpv4_reservation` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_kea_dhcpv4_reservation--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_kea_dhcpv4_reservation--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_kea_dhcpv4_reservation` resource. You will seldom need to specify this ---
Puppet will usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_kea_dhcpv4_subnet"></a>`opn_kea_dhcpv4_subnet`

Manages KEA DHCPv4 subnets on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "subnet_cidr@device_name", where
"subnet_cidr" is the CIDR notation (e.g. '192.168.1.0/24') that uniquely
identifies the subnet and "device_name" corresponds to a YAML config file
managed by the opn class at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

The subnet CIDR has a UniqueConstraint in the OPNsense model, so it serves
as the natural identifier. Reservations autorequire their parent subnet.

#### Examples

##### Create a DHCPv4 subnet

```puppet
opn_kea_dhcpv4_subnet { '192.168.1.0/24@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'description'             => 'LAN DHCP',
    'option_data_autocollect' => '1',
    'option_data'             => {
      'routers'              => '192.168.1.1',
      'domain_name_servers'  => '8.8.8.8,8.8.4.4',
      'domain_name'          => 'example.com',
    },
    'pools' => '192.168.1.100 - 192.168.1.200',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_kea_dhcpv4_subnet` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_kea_dhcpv4_subnet--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_kea_dhcpv4_subnet--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_kea_dhcpv4_subnet` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet
will usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

KEA DHCPv6 'general', 'lexpire' and 'ha' sections. The `config` hash
is passed directly to the OPNsense API without modification.

```puppet
    'general' => {
```

Manages KEA DHCPv6 prefix delegation pools on an OPNsense device via the

The resource title uses the format "description@device_name", where

Manages KEA DHCPv6 reservations on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense

The resource title uses the format "description@device_name", where
"description" uniquely identifies the reservation and "device_name"
corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class at

The subnet relation field accepts a subnet CIDR which is automatically

##### Create a DHCPv6 reservation

```puppet
opn_kea_dhcpv6_reservation { 'Mail Server@opnsense.example.com':
```

### <a name="opn_kea_dhcpv6_subnet"></a>`opn_kea_dhcpv6_subnet`

Manages KEA DHCPv6 subnets on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "subnet_cidr@device_name", where
"subnet_cidr" is the CIDR notation (e.g. 'fd00::/64') that uniquely
identifies the subnet and "device_name" corresponds to a YAML config file
managed by the opn class at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

The subnet CIDR has a UniqueConstraint in the OPNsense model, so it serves
as the natural identifier. Reservations and PD pools autorequire their
parent subnet.

#### Examples

##### Create a DHCPv6 subnet

```puppet
opn_kea_dhcpv6_subnet { 'fd00::/64@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'description' => 'LAN DHCPv6',
    'interface'   => 'lan',
    'option_data' => {
      'dns_servers' => 'fd00::1',
    },
    'pools' => 'fd00::100 - fd00::200',
  },
}
```
