### <a name="opn_node_exporter"></a>`opn_node_exporter`

Manages Prometheus Node Exporter settings on an OPNsense device via the
OPNsense REST API.

The resource title is the OPNsense device name, corresponding to a YAML
config file managed by the opn class at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

This is a singleton resource -- one per OPNsense device. The `config` hash
is passed directly to the OPNsense API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Configure Node Exporter

```puppet
opn_node_exporter { 'opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'enabled'       => '1',
    'listenaddress' => '0.0.0.0',
    'listenport'    => '9100',
    'cpu'           => '1',
    'exec'          => '1',
    'filesystem'    => '1',
    'loadavg'       => '1',
    'meminfo'       => '1',
    'netdev'        => '1',
    'time'          => '1',
    'devstat'       => '1',
    'interrupts'    => '0',
    'ntp'           => '0',
    'zfs'           => '1',
  },
}
```

##### Disable Node Exporter

```puppet
opn_node_exporter { 'opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => absent,
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_node_exporter` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_node_exporter--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_node_exporter--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_node_exporter` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_openvpn_cso"></a>`opn_openvpn_cso`

Manages OpenVPN client-specific overrides (CSO) on an OPNsense device
via the OPNsense REST API (MVC model).

The resource title uses the format "common_name@device_name", where
"common_name" uniquely identifies the override entry and "device_name"
corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

The servers relation field accepts instance descriptions which are
automatically resolved to UUIDs via the IdResolver.

#### Examples

##### Create an OpenVPN client override

```puppet
opn_openvpn_cso { 'client1@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'servers'          => 'roadwarrior-server',
    'tunnel_network'   => '10.8.1.0/24',
    'local_network'    => '192.168.1.0/24',
    'enabled'          => '1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_openvpn_cso` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_openvpn_cso--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_openvpn_cso--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_openvpn_cso` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_openvpn_instance"></a>`opn_openvpn_instance`

Manages OpenVPN instances on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API
(MVC model).

The resource title uses the format "description@device_name", where
"description" uniquely identifies the instance and "device_name"
corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

The 'password' field is intentionally excluded from idempotency
comparison because it contains secret material.

Volatile fields (vpnid) are auto-assigned by OPNsense and excluded from
idempotency checks.

The tls_key relation field accepts a static key description which is
automatically resolved to a UUID via the IdResolver.

#### Examples

##### Create an OpenVPN server instance

```puppet
opn_openvpn_instance { 'roadwarrior-server@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'role'           => 'server',
    'proto'          => 'udp',
    'port'           => '1194',
    'server'         => '10.8.0.0/24',
    'tls_key'        => 'my-tls-auth-key',
    'enabled'        => '1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_openvpn_instance` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_openvpn_instance--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_openvpn_instance--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_openvpn_instance` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet
will usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_openvpn_statickey"></a>`opn_openvpn_statickey`

Manages OpenVPN static keys on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense
REST API (MVC model).

The resource title uses the format "description@device_name", where
"description" uniquely identifies the static key and "device_name"
corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

#### Examples

##### Create an OpenVPN static key

```puppet
opn_openvpn_statickey { 'my-tls-auth-key@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'mode' => 'auth',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_openvpn_statickey` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_openvpn_statickey--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_openvpn_statickey--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_openvpn_statickey` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet
will usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_plugin"></a>`opn_plugin`

Manages plugins/packages on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "plugin_name@device_name", where
"device_name" corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class
at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

Plugin names follow the OPNsense package naming convention (e.g. "os-haproxy",
"os-acme-client", "os-zerotier"). The OPNsense firmware API handles
installation and removal; no additional configuration is required.

Note: Install/remove operations are executed immediately but may take some
time to complete on the OPNsense device (package manager runs asynchronously).

#### Examples

##### Install HAProxy plugin

```puppet
opn_plugin { 'os-haproxy@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
}
```

##### Remove a plugin

```puppet
opn_plugin { 'os-acme-client@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => absent,
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_plugin` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_plugin--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_plugin--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_plugin` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will usually
discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_route"></a>`opn_route`

Manages static routes on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "description@device_name", where
"description" uniquely identifies the static route and "device_name"
corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

Note: The description must be unique per device. Two routes with the same
description on the same device will cause unpredictable behaviour.

All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The `config`
hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Manage a static route

```puppet
opn_route { 'Server network@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'network'  => '10.0.0.0/24',
    'gateway'  => 'Wan_DHCP',
    'disabled'  => '0',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_route` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_route--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_route--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_route` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will usually
discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_snapshot"></a>`opn_snapshot`

Manages ZFS snapshots on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "name@device_name", where "name"
uniquely identifies the snapshot and "device_name" corresponds to a YAML
config file managed by the opn class at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

The `active` property controls whether a snapshot is the active boot
target. Setting `active => true` triggers the activate endpoint. A
snapshot cannot be deactivated (another snapshot must be activated
instead). Active snapshots cannot be deleted.

All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The `config`
hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Create a snapshot with a note

```puppet
opn_snapshot { 'pre-upgrade@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'note' => 'Snapshot before upgrade',
  },
}
```

##### Create and activate a snapshot

```puppet
opn_snapshot { 'stable@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  active => true,
  config => {
    'note' => 'Stable configuration',
  },
}
```

#### Properties

The following properties are available in the `opn_snapshot` type.

##### `active`

Valid values: `true`, `false`

Whether this snapshot is the active boot target.

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_snapshot` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_snapshot--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_snapshot--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_snapshot` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_syslog"></a>`opn_syslog`

Manages syslog destinations on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "description@device_name", where
"description" uniquely identifies the syslog destination and "device_name"
corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

Note: The description must be unique per device. Two syslog destinations
with the same description on the same device will cause unpredictable
behaviour.

All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The `config`
hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Forward logs to a remote syslog server

```puppet
opn_syslog { 'Central syslog@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'transport' => 'udp4',
    'hostname'  => 'syslog.example.com',
    'port'      => '514',
    'level'     => 'info,notice,warn,err,crit,alert,emerg',
    'enabled'   => '1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_syslog` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_syslog--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_syslog--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_syslog` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will usually
discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_trust_ca"></a>`opn_trust_ca`

Manages trust Certificate Authorities (CAs) on an OPNsense device via the
OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "descr@device_name", where "descr"
uniquely identifies the CA and "device_name" corresponds to a YAML config
file managed by the opn class at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

Many fields (action, key_type, digest, etc.) are only used during initial
creation and are ignored during idempotency checks. These volatile fields
are sent on create but excluded from update operations.

All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The `config`
hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Import an external CA

```puppet
opn_trust_ca { 'My Root CA@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'action'      => 'import',
    'crt_payload' => '-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----\n...\n-----END CERTIFICATE-----',
  },
}
```

##### Create an internal CA

```puppet
opn_trust_ca { 'Internal CA@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'action'       => 'internal',
    'key_type'     => 'RSA',
    'digest'       => 'SHA256',
    'lifetime'     => '3650',
    'country'      => 'DE',
    'state'        => 'Berlin',
    'city'         => 'Berlin',
    'organization' => 'Example Corp',
    'commonname'   => 'Internal CA',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_trust_ca` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_trust_ca--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_trust_ca--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_trust_ca` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_trust_cert"></a>`opn_trust_cert`

Manages trust certificates on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "descr@device_name", where "descr"
uniquely identifies the certificate and "device_name" corresponds to a YAML
config file managed by the opn class at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

Many fields (action, key_type, digest, etc.) are only used during initial
creation and are ignored during idempotency checks. These volatile fields
are sent on create but excluded from update operations.

All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The `config`
hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Import an external certificate

```puppet
opn_trust_cert { 'web.example.com@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'action'      => 'import',
    'crt_payload' => '-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----\n...\n-----END CERTIFICATE-----',
    'prv_payload' => '-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----\n...\n-----END PRIVATE KEY-----',
  },
}
```

##### Create an internal certificate signed by a CA

```puppet
opn_trust_cert { 'Internal Cert@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'action'       => 'internal',
    'caref'        => '<ca-uuid>',
    'key_type'     => 'RSA',
    'digest'       => 'SHA256',
    'lifetime'     => '365',
    'country'      => 'DE',
    'commonname'   => 'internal.example.com',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_trust_cert` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_trust_cert--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_trust_cert--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_trust_cert` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_trust_crl"></a>`opn_trust_crl`

Manages Certificate Revocation Lists (CRLs) on an OPNsense device via the
OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "ca_description@device_name", where
"ca_description" is the description of the CA to which this CRL belongs.
The provider resolves the CA description to the internal caref identifier.

Each CA can have at most one CRL. The CRL is identified by its CA
reference, not by a UUID.

All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The `config`
hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Create an internal CRL for a CA

```puppet
opn_trust_crl { 'My Root CA@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'descr'     => 'CRL for My Root CA',
    'lifetime'  => '9999',
    'crlmethod' => 'internal',
  },
}
```

##### Import an existing CRL

```puppet
opn_trust_crl { 'External CA@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'descr'     => 'Imported CRL',
    'crlmethod' => 'existing',
    'text'      => '-----BEGIN X509 CRL-----\n...\n-----END X509 CRL-----',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_trust_crl` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_trust_crl--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_trust_crl--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_trust_crl` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_tunable"></a>`opn_tunable`

Manages system tunables (sysctl) on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense
REST API.

The resource title uses the format "tunable@device_name", where "tunable"
is the sysctl variable name (e.g. "kern.maxproc") and "device_name"
corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class at
/etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The `config`
hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Set a system tunable

```puppet
opn_tunable { 'kern.maxproc@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'value'       => '4096',
    'description' => 'Maximum number of processes',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_tunable` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_tunable--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_tunable--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_tunable` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_user"></a>`opn_user`

Manages local users on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "username@device_name", where
"device_name" corresponds to a YAML config file managed by the opn class
at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

All configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API. The `config`
hash is passed through to the API without modification.

#### Examples

##### Create a local user

```puppet
opn_user { 'jdoe@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'password'    => '$2y$11$...',
    'description' => 'John Doe',
    'email'       => 'jdoe@example.com',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_user` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_user--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_user--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_user` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will usually
discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_zabbix_agent"></a>`opn_zabbix_agent`

Manages Zabbix Agent settings on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title is the OPNsense device name, corresponding to a YAML
config file managed by the opn class at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

This is a singleton resource — one per OPNsense device. It maps to the
Zabbix Agent plugin (os-zabbix-agent) settings. The `config` hash is passed
directly to the OPNsense API without modification.

Userparameters and aliases are managed by separate resource types:
opn_zabbix_agent_userparameter and opn_zabbix_agent_alias.

Requires the os-zabbix-agent plugin to be installed on the device.

#### Examples

##### Configure Zabbix Agent

```puppet
opn_zabbix_agent { 'opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'settings' => {
      'main' => {
        'enabled'    => '1',
        'serverList' => 'zabbix.example.com',
        'listenPort' => '10050',
      },
    },
  },
}
```

##### Disable Zabbix Agent

```puppet
opn_zabbix_agent { 'opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => absent,
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_zabbix_agent` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_zabbix_agent--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_zabbix_agent--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_zabbix_agent` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_zabbix_agent_alias"></a>`opn_zabbix_agent_alias`

Manages Zabbix Agent Alias entries on an OPNsense device via the
OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "key@device_name". The "key" portion
(before "@") is the Zabbix Alias key exactly as it will appear in the
OPNsense/Zabbix configuration (e.g. "ping" or "cpu.load[*]"). It serves
as the unique identifier for the resource and is always set from the
title — specifying a different "key" value inside the `config` hash has
no effect.

To rename an alias key, rename the resource title. If an existing entry
must be renamed, declare the old title with `ensure => absent` and add a
new resource with the new title.

All other configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API.

Requires the os-zabbix-agent plugin to be installed on the device.

#### Examples

##### Define an alias (key comes from the title, not from config)

```puppet
opn_zabbix_agent_alias { 'ping@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'enabled'      => '1',
    'sourceKey'    => 'icmpping',
    'acceptParams' => '0',
  },
}
```

##### Rename an alias key

```puppet
# Remove the old entry
opn_zabbix_agent_alias { 'ping@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => absent,
}
# Create the new entry with the new key as the resource title
opn_zabbix_agent_alias { 'icmp.ping@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'enabled'      => '1',
    'sourceKey'    => 'icmpping',
    'acceptParams' => '0',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_zabbix_agent_alias` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_zabbix_agent_alias--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_zabbix_agent_alias--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_zabbix_agent_alias` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet
will usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_zabbix_agent_userparameter"></a>`opn_zabbix_agent_userparameter`

Manages Zabbix Agent UserParameter entries on an OPNsense device via the
OPNsense REST API.

The resource title uses the format "key@device_name". The "key" portion
(before "@") is the Zabbix UserParameter key exactly as it will appear in
the OPNsense/Zabbix configuration (e.g. "custom.uptime" or
"system.cpu.load[all,avg5]"). It serves as the unique identifier for the
resource and is always set from the title — specifying a different "key"
value inside the `config` hash has no effect.

To rename a userparameter key, rename the resource title. If an existing
entry must be renamed, declare the old title with `ensure => absent` and
add a new resource with the new title.

All other configuration validation is delegated to the OPNsense API.

Requires the os-zabbix-agent plugin to be installed on the device.

#### Examples

##### Define a userparameter (key comes from the title, not from config)

```puppet
opn_zabbix_agent_userparameter { 'custom.uptime@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'enabled'      => '1',
    'command'      => '/usr/bin/uptime',
    'acceptParams' => '0',
  },
}
```

##### Rename a userparameter key

```puppet
# Remove the old entry
opn_zabbix_agent_userparameter { 'custom.uptime@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => absent,
}
# Create the new entry with the new key as the resource title
opn_zabbix_agent_userparameter { 'custom.uptime[*]@opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'enabled'      => '1',
    'command'      => '/usr/bin/uptime',
    'acceptParams' => '1',
  },
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_zabbix_agent_userparameter` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_zabbix_agent_userparameter--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_zabbix_agent_userparameter--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_zabbix_agent_userparameter` resource. You will seldom need to specify this ---
Puppet will usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.

### <a name="opn_zabbix_proxy"></a>`opn_zabbix_proxy`

Manages Zabbix Proxy settings on an OPNsense device via the OPNsense REST API.

The resource title is the OPNsense device name, corresponding to a YAML
config file managed by the opn class at /etc/puppet/opn/<device_name>.yaml.

This is a singleton resource — one per OPNsense device. It maps to the
Zabbix Proxy plugin (os-zabbix-proxy) general settings. The `config` hash
is passed directly to the OPNsense API without modification.

Requires the os-zabbix-proxy plugin to be installed on the device.

#### Examples

##### Configure Zabbix Proxy

```puppet
opn_zabbix_proxy { 'opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => present,
  config => {
    'enabled'    => '1',
    'server'     => 'zabbix.example.com',
    'serverport' => '10051',
    'hostname'   => 'opnsense-proxy',
  },
}
```

##### Disable Zabbix Proxy

```puppet
opn_zabbix_proxy { 'opnsense.example.com':
  ensure => absent,
}
```

#### Parameters

The following parameters are available in the `opn_zabbix_proxy` type.

* [`provider`](#-opn_zabbix_proxy--provider)

##### <a name="-opn_zabbix_proxy--provider"></a>`provider`

The specific backend to use for this `opn_zabbix_proxy` resource. You will seldom need to specify this --- Puppet will
usually discover the appropriate provider for your platform.
